summary: Hindu Wedding Invitation Website Builder — a full-stack web app where authenticated users (via Internet Identity) create cinematic, full-page Hindu wedding invitation websites. Features 12 richly styled templates (8 original + 4 new premium: Mughal Garden, Vedic Fire, Royal Navy Zari, Lotus Bloom) with light/dark variants. Login-based system: only authenticated creators can create/edit invites and view RSVP responses. Home page transforms into a personal 'My Invitations' dashboard when logged in. 'View RSVP Responses' button hidden from guests — only visible to the logged-in creator on their own invite page. Anonymous invite creation disabled; old anonymous invites no longer editable. Email field in RSVP form is optional. Separate bride/groom photo uploads, gallery multi-upload with drag & drop, background music upload, live countdown timer, animated events timeline, RSVP form with confetti burst, shareable unique URLs, QR code generation, OG meta tags with cache-busting, WYSIWYG text editing, and full cinematic visual effects (floating frames, parallax, mandala reveals, drifting petals, glassmorphism).
architectural_notes: Google Maps integration uses 'Open in Maps' link button (no embedded map widget)
OG image uses uploaded cover photo directly with cache-busting (?v=timestamp); no server-side compositing
Photos stored at original quality; responsive srcset and WebP fallback served without lossy downscaling; max 20MB/image

IC canister message size limit is ~2MB (2,097,152 bytes); Base64-encoded images sent inline in createInvite/updateInvite payloads exceed this limit and cause HTTP 413 errors. Images must be chunked, compressed before Base64 encoding, or stored/referenced externally rather than embedded inline in the canister call payload.
Authentication uses Internet Identity; invite creation, editing, and RSVP response viewing are gated behind login. Anonymous callers cannot create or modify invites.
Each invite record is associated with the creator's Internet Identity principal; only that principal can edit the invite or view its RSVP responses.
known_issues: Publishing invite fails with HTTP 413 (payload too large: 2.7MB > 2MB IC limit) when Base64-encoded photos are included inline in the canister message
Background music does not autoplay when invitation page is opened
Publishing an invite and opening the invitation page are both slow (performance issue)
Bride/groom photo frame overlaps/shows in the middle of the photo instead of acting as a border around it
Publish button not working — invite is not being saved; createInvite/updateInvite flow not completing and shareable URL not returned
RSVP section text color blends into background, making form content unreadable
Edit flow does not pre-fill saved invite data — all fields are empty when editing an existing invite from the dashboard
'Actor not available' error shown when clicking Publish Invitation — actor client not yet initialized at the time of the canister call

existing_features.title: Build a multi-step creator flow in React: Step 1 — Choose template (8 cards with live mini-preview, light/dark toggle per card). Step 2 — Enter couple details (bride name, groom name, wedding date/time, tagline). Step 3 — Upload bride photo and groom photo via separate upload buttons (JPG/PNG/WebP, max 20 MB each; show preview; preserve original; generate responsive srcset with WebP fallback). Step 4 — Add events (name, date/time, venue, address, Google Maps URL, optional image; allow multiple events). Step 5 — Upload gallery images (drag & drop, reorder, preview; no auto-compression) and optional background music (MP3/OGG). Step 6 — Publish: display the shareable URL and QR code.

Implement 8 fully styled invitation page templates as React components: Royal Rajasthani (deep crimson, gold filigree), Temple Mandala (intricate mandala SVG overlay, ochre/maroon), Dark Cosmic (near-black, starfield, gold constellation lines), Floral Marigold (marigold orange, lush floral borders), South Indian Bronze (bronze/copper tones, Kolam patterns), Minimal Sanskrit (cream/ivory, refined typography, subtle Om watermark), Sunset Beach (warm sunset gradient, wave motifs), 3D Floating Divine (layered 3D depth, divine purple/gold). Each template has a light and a dark variant toggled via a prop. All templates are full-bleed cinematic pages — no boxed card layouts.

Add cinematic visual effects to all invitation templates: (a) Floating photo frames — bride and groom photos in decorative frames with a continuous subtle 3D bobbing CSS animation. (b) Mouse-tilt hero — the hero section tilts slightly on mouse/pointer move using a perspective transform. (c) Parallax layers — background patterns and decoration layers scroll at different rates. (d) Animated mandala reveal — a mandala SVG that rotates in and expands on page load. (e) Gold filigree SVG masks over photo frames and section dividers. (f) Drifting petals/incense particle layer — CSS/canvas particles (marigold petals or incense smoke dots) drifting across the viewport. (g) Glassmorphism info panels for event cards and RSVP section. All effects must be disabled or reduced when the reduce-motion toggle is on.

Implement a live countdown timer in the hero section of every invitation template. Display days, hours, minutes, and seconds as animated flip-card or glowing numeric counters. When the countdown reaches zero, replace the timer with a glowing 'The celebration begins!' message. The timer updates every second via setInterval.

Build an events timeline section in the invitation page. Render each event as an animated timeline card (alternating left/right on desktop, stacked on mobile) with: event name, date/time, venue name, address, an 'Open in Maps' button that opens the pasted Google Maps URL in a new tab, and an optional event image. Timeline cards animate in on scroll using IntersectionObserver (disabled when reduce-motion is on). Use glassmorphism card styling.

Build an RSVP form section with fields: full name, phone, email, attending (Yes/No radio), guest count (number input), meal preference (dropdown: Vegetarian, Non-Vegetarian, Vegan, No Preference), and a message textarea. On successful submission, trigger a full-screen confetti/flower petal burst animation and display a customizable thank-you message (editable by the invite creator). Form submits to the backend submitRSVP endpoint. Disable the form after successful submission to prevent duplicates.

Implement shareable invite URLs and QR code generation. Each invite is accessible at a route like /invite/:id. On the publish step of the creator flow, display the full shareable URL and generate a QR code (using a client-side QR library, e.g. qrcode.react) that encodes the URL. Provide a copy-to-clipboard button for the URL.

Implement OG meta tags on each invitation page for social sharing. Inject into the document head: og:title (couple names + wedding date), og:description (tagline or event summary), og:image (cover photo URL with ?v=timestamp cache-busting parameter), og:image:width (1200), og:image:height (630), twitter:card (summary_large_image). Use a default fallback image at /assets/generated/og-fallback.dim_1200x630.png when no cover photo is uploaded. Use React Helmet or equivalent for dynamic head injection.

Implement background music playback in the invitation page. Display a visible floating play/pause toggle button (bottom-right corner). Music only starts after the first user interaction (click/tap anywhere on the page). Loop the audio. Accept MP3 and OGG formats. Respect the reduce-motion toggle (do not auto-animate the music button when reduce-motion is on).

Implement WYSIWYG inline text editing for invitation pages. Allow the creator to click any text element (couple names, tagline, section headings, body text) and edit it in place. Provide a floating toolbar with font family selector (curated Hindu-appropriate pairings: Cormorant Garamond + EB Garamond, Playfair Display + Lato, Noto Serif Devanagari + Open Sans, Cinzel + Raleway), font size, bold/italic, and color picker. Also provide color presets: Gilded Saffron (#F4A832), Royal Marigold (#E8731A), Temple Red (#C0392B), Deep Indigo (#3B1F6E), Ivory (#FAF3E0). Allow toggling entire sections (couple story, gallery, travel/accommodation, dress code, blessings) on/off.

Add a live preview panel in the creator/editor flow. Show a toggleable mobile (390px wide) and desktop (1280px wide) preview of the invitation rendered in an iframe or scaled container. Include a reduce-motion accessibility toggle that, when enabled, disables all CSS animations and transitions on the preview and the published page.

Build a home/landing page for the app. Show a hero section explaining the product, a gallery of the 8 sample invites as clickable cards, and a prominent 'Create Your Invitation' CTA button that starts the creator flow. Include a brief how-it-works section (3 steps: Choose template → Customize → Share).

Apply a rich cinematic visual theme across the entire app (creator flow, home page, and invitation pages). Use deep jewel tones (saffron, marigold, crimson, gold, ivory) with ornate Hindu-inspired decorative motifs as SVG accents. Typography should use Cormorant Garamond for headings and EB Garamond for body. The creator UI should feel premium and editorial — dark deep-maroon/ivory surfaces with gold accents, subtle grain texture, and animated section transitions. Enforce the theme consistently across all pages.

Fix bride photo, groom photo, and gallery images not rendering in the published invitation page. Photos are uploaded and stored as object URLs (blob: URLs) during the creator flow but these object URLs are ephemeral — they become invalid after the session ends or the page is refreshed. The fix must persist photo data by converting uploaded images to Base64 data URLs before storing them in the backend invite record, so that photos are retrievable and renderable on the published /invite/:id page across sessions and devices.

Update the frontend photo upload steps (StepPhotoUpload.tsx for bride/groom/cover photos and StepGalleryMusic.tsx for gallery images) to convert uploaded files to Base64 data URLs using FileReader before storing them in the creator form state. Replace all object URL (URL.createObjectURL) usage for photos that need to be persisted with Base64 data URL conversion, so the form state submitted to the backend contains persistent Base64 strings instead of ephemeral blob URLs.

Update the frontend photo upload flow to use the new chunked upload endpoints instead of embedding Base64 data inline in the publish payload. In StepPhotoUpload.tsx and StepGalleryMusic.tsx, after a user selects an image file, split the Base64 data URL into chunks of at most 1MB each and call uploadImageChunk sequentially for each chunk, then call finalizeImage to commit the image. Store only a reference token or the fieldKey (not the full Base64 string) in the creator form state. When the CreateInvitePage assembles the final InvitePayload for createInvite/updateInvite, omit raw image data — images are already stored via the chunked upload flow. Show per-image upload progress during chunked upload.

Fix bride and groom photos being hidden behind the decorative filigree frame overlay in FloatingPhotoFrame.tsx and BaseTemplate.tsx. The photo image must render visibly inside the frame at full size — ensure the frame overlay (SVG filigree or border element) is positioned on top of the photo using z-index layering (photo at z-index base, frame overlay above), and that the photo container does not have overflow:hidden or opacity/visibility rules that clip or obscure the photo. The photo must be clearly visible with the decorative frame appearing as a border around it, not covering it.

Update BackgroundMusic.tsx to autoplay the background music as soon as the invitation page is opened. Remove the requirement for a separate first-click-anywhere interaction before playback starts. Instead, attempt to call audio.play() immediately on mount using a useEffect. If the browser blocks autoplay (promise rejection), fall back to playing on the first user interaction (click/tap anywhere on the page). The floating play/pause button must remain visible and functional. Loop the audio continuously. Respect the reduce-motion toggle for button animation only.

Optimize the invite publishing flow to reduce the time taken when the user clicks Publish in the creator flow. Specifically: (1) In the backend main.mo, ensure finalizeImage chunk assembly is O(n) and does not perform redundant iterations. (2) In the frontend CreateInvitePage.tsx and StepPhotoUpload.tsx/StepGalleryMusic.tsx, parallelize independent chunked upload calls where possible (upload bride photo chunks and groom photo chunks concurrently rather than sequentially). (3) Show a meaningful per-step progress indicator during publishing so the user understands the app is working. (4) After createInvite/updateInvite succeeds, navigate to the success/publish step immediately without additional round-trips.

Optimize the invitation page load time at /invite/:id. In InvitePage.tsx, ensure the invite data fetch via React Query is efficient: set an appropriate staleTime (at least 60 seconds) so repeat visits do not re-fetch unnecessarily. Lazy-load the template component (already lazy per codebase) and ensure the loading skeleton is shown immediately. In BaseTemplate.tsx, defer rendering of heavy sections (gallery, events timeline, RSVP form) until they are near the viewport using IntersectionObserver or React.lazy with Suspense, so the hero section paints first. Avoid blocking the initial render on non-critical assets (music, gallery images).

In FloatingPhotoFrame.tsx and BaseTemplate.tsx, remove the decorative filigree frame/overlay SVG element that renders on top of or in the middle of the bride and groom photos. Replace it with a premium CSS border/boundary styled directly on the photo container — use a multi-layered border approach (e.g., a thick solid or double gold/saffron border with a subtle inset shadow and an outer glow) that frames the photo from the outside without any element overlapping the photo image itself. The photo must be fully visible at all times. The border style should feel premium and on-theme (gold tones, rounded or slightly ornate corners using border-radius or a clip-path, consistent with the Hindu wedding aesthetic). The floating 3D bobbing animation on the photo container should be retained.

Add a WhatsApp sharing button to the invitation page (InvitePage.tsx or ShareSection.tsx). The button should be visible on the published invitation page at /invite/:id. When clicked, it opens a WhatsApp share link in a new tab using the format https://wa.me/?text=<encoded message> where the message includes the couple names, wedding date, and the full shareable invitation URL. Style the button to match the app's premium theme (gold/saffron accent, Cormorant Garamond or Cinzel typography). Place it near the existing share/QR section or as a floating button.

Fix the publish button in StepPublish.tsx so that the createInvite/updateInvite backend call completes successfully and persists the invite record. Debug the full publish pipeline in CreateInvitePage.tsx: ensure the InvitePayload is assembled correctly (no raw Base64 image data inline — images are already stored via the chunked upload flow), the actor call is awaited and errors are caught and surfaced to the user, and on success the invite ID and shareable URL are returned and displayed in the publish step. The publish button must not silently fail or hang.
Add a new route /invite/:id/responses in the frontend router (App.tsx) that renders a new RSVPResponsesPage component. The route must coexist with the existing /invite/:id route without affecting it.

Create a new frontend page component RSVPResponsesPage.tsx at frontend/src/pages/RSVPResponsesPage.tsx. It fetches the invite details (to display couple names and wedding date in the heading) and all RSVP responses for the invite ID from the URL param using React Query. Display a summary bar at the top showing: total responses, total attending (Yes), total not attending (No), and total guest count. Below the summary, render a full responsive table listing each respondent with columns: Name, Attending, Guests, Meal Preference, Message, and Submitted At. On mobile, collapse the table to stacked cards. Show a loading skeleton while data is fetching and an empty-state message ('No responses yet — check back soon!') if the responses array is empty.

Add a useRSVPResponses React Query hook in frontend/src/hooks/useQueries.ts that calls the getRSVPResponses(inviteId) backend endpoint and returns the list of RSVP records. Set staleTime to at least 30 seconds.

On the published invitation page (InvitePage.tsx or within BaseTemplate.tsx / ShareSection.tsx), add a visible 'View RSVP Responses' link/button that navigates to /invite/:id/responses. Style the button to match the app's premium theme (gold/saffron accent, Cormorant Garamond typography). Place it near the existing share/QR section at the bottom of the invitation page.

Add a `getMyInvites` React Query hook in `frontend/src/hooks/useQueries.ts` that calls the backend `getMyInvites()` endpoint using the authenticated actor. The query should only run when the user is authenticated. Set `staleTime` to at least 30 seconds.

Transform `frontend/src/pages/LandingPage.tsx` so that when the user is logged in (via Internet Identity), the page renders a 'My Invitations' personal dashboard instead of the marketing landing page. The dashboard must: (a) show a greeting with the user's principal (abbreviated) and a logout button, (b) list all of the user's invites fetched via the `useMyInvites` hook, each displayed as a card with the couple names, wedding date, template name, and two action buttons — 'Edit' (navigates to the creator flow pre-filled with that invite's data) and 'View RSVP Responses' (navigates to `/invite/:id/responses`), (c) include a prominent 'Create New Invitation' CTA button, (d) show a loading skeleton while invites are loading and an empty-state message ('No invitations yet — create your first one!') when the list is empty. When the user is logged out, the page continues to show the existing marketing landing page with the 'Create Your Invitation' CTA — but that CTA must now trigger Internet Identity login before navigating to the creator flow.

Gate the creator flow in `frontend/src/pages/CreateInvitePage.tsx` so that an unauthenticated user is redirected to the landing page (or shown a login prompt) instead of being able to create or edit an invite. If the user navigates directly to the create/edit URL without being logged in, display an authentication prompt or redirect to the home page.

Gate the RSVP responses page in `frontend/src/pages/RSVPResponsesPage.tsx` so that only the authenticated creator of the specific invite can view its RSVP responses. On load, fetch the invite's creator principal via `getInviteCreator(id)` and compare it to the current authenticated user's principal. If the user is not authenticated or is not the creator, show an 'Access Denied' message with a login prompt rather than displaying the response data.

In `frontend/src/components/invitation/ShareSection.tsx` (or `InvitePage.tsx`), hide the 'View RSVP Responses' button from guests. The button should only render when the currently authenticated user's principal matches the invite's creator principal (obtained via `getInviteCreator`). Guests and unauthenticated users must not see this button.

Make the email field in the RSVP form (`frontend/src/components/invitation/RSVPForm.tsx`) optional — remove the `required` validation constraint from the email field. The field should remain visible in the form but submission must succeed even when the email is left blank.

Add 4 new premium wedding invitation templates as React components: (1) Mughal Garden — lush Mughal-era aesthetic with deep emerald greens, ornate arch motifs, intricate floral jali SVG patterns, and gold calligraphy-style typography; (2) Vedic Fire — warm amber and deep crimson evoking sacred fire ceremony, agni flame SVG motifs, geometric Vedic yantras, and ochre/saffron gradients; (3) Royal Navy Zari — deep navy blue with gold zari-embroidery-inspired SVG borders, regal crest motif, ivory and silver accents; (4) Lotus Bloom — soft pink and white lotus theme with layered petal SVG accents, delicate pastel gradients, and a serene water-lily motif. Each template must have a light and a dark variant toggled via a prop, be full-bleed cinematic pages (no boxed card layouts), include a 'Premium' badge on the template selector card, and delegate rendering to `BaseTemplate` following the same pattern as existing templates. Register all 4 in `frontend/src/lib/templateRegistry.ts`.

Update `frontend/src/components/layout/AppLayout.tsx` to display an Internet Identity login/logout button in the sticky header. When logged out, show a 'Login' button that triggers Internet Identity authentication. When logged in, show the user's abbreviated principal and a 'Logout' button. Use the existing `useInternetIdentity` hook for auth state and actions.

Fix text visibility in the RSVP form section across all invitation templates. Update text colors for all form elements — labels, input fields, textarea, dropdown/select, radio buttons, placeholder text, and helper text — so they have strong contrast against their backgrounds in both light and dark template variants. Specifically, in RSVPForm.tsx ensure input/textarea/select elements use a dark text color (e.g., near-black or deep-maroon) on light backgrounds and a near-white or ivory text color on dark backgrounds. Labels must also be clearly legible. Do not rely solely on the inherited template color, but explicitly set text colors on each form element to guarantee readability.

Fix the edit flow in CreateInvitePage.tsx so that when a user clicks 'Edit' on an existing invite from the dashboard (navigating to the creator flow with an invite ID in the URL or route params), the form state is fully pre-populated with the saved invite data fetched from the backend. On mount, if an existing invite ID is present, fetch the invite record using the existing getInvite query and initialize all form state fields from the returned record — including: selected template ID and dark/light variant, couple details (bride name, groom name, wedding date/time, tagline, RSVP thank-you message), events list (all event objects with name, date/time, venue, address, Google Maps URL), gallery image references, and background music reference. Image fields that were stored via chunked upload should be represented by their stored reference tokens or keys, not re-uploaded. The form must open at Step 1 (or the first step) pre-filled, allowing the user to navigate through steps and see their previously saved data.

In BaseTemplate.tsx, add support for a `rsvpBackground` prop (a CSS color string or Tailwind class) that is applied as the background of the RSVP section container. If no `rsvpBackground` is provided, fall back to the existing glassmorphism default.

Update RoyalRajasthaniTemplate.tsx to pass a deep maroon tint (e.g., rgba(80, 10, 20, 0.88)) as the RSVP section background via the `rsvpBackground` prop on BaseTemplate, ensuring strong contrast for form labels and inputs in both light and dark variants.
Update TempleMandalaTemplate.tsx to pass a rich ochre/maroon tint (e.g., rgba(90, 40, 10, 0.90)) as the RSVP section background that complements its burnt orange and ochre palette.
Update DarkCosmicTemplate.tsx to pass a near-black deep navy tint (e.g., rgba(5, 5, 30, 0.92)) as the RSVP section background that fits its starfield/cosmic palette.
Update FloralMarigoldTemplate.tsx to pass a deep amber/burnt orange tint (e.g., rgba(130, 50, 5, 0.88)) as the RSVP section background that fits its marigold palette.
Update SouthIndianBronzeTemplate.tsx to pass a dark bronze/copper tint (e.g., rgba(60, 30, 10, 0.90)) as the RSVP section background that fits its bronze/copper palette.

Update MinimalSanskritTemplate.tsx to pass a warm parchment/cream tint (e.g., rgba(245, 238, 220, 0.95)) as the RSVP section background that fits its ivory/cream palette, and ensure form text uses dark near-black tones for contrast.
Update SunsetBeachTemplate.tsx to pass a deep sunset coral/terracotta tint (e.g., rgba(120, 50, 20, 0.88)) as the RSVP section background that fits its warm sunset gradient palette.
Update ThreeDFloatingDivineTemplate.tsx to pass a deep divine purple tint (e.g., rgba(30, 5, 60, 0.92)) as the RSVP section background that fits its purple/gold palette.
Update MughalGardenTemplate.tsx to pass a deep emerald green tint (e.g., rgba(5, 50, 30, 0.90)) as the RSVP section background that fits its Mughal emerald/gold palette.
Update VedicFireTemplate.tsx to pass a deep crimson/amber tint (e.g., rgba(100, 20, 5, 0.90)) as the RSVP section background that fits its sacred fire amber/crimson palette.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in RoyalRajasthaniTemplate.tsx. Replace the existing generic golden patterns with unique, intricate Rajasthani filigree jali lattice panels and paisley (boteh) motifs rendered as inline SVGs. The jali pattern should tile as a fine geometric grid overlay across the hero and body sections. Paisley clusters should frame section dividers. Add a subtle radial depth shadow overlay beneath the main hero layer to create a 3D cinematic depth effect.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in TempleMandalaTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with layered concentric mandala geometry: a large primary mandala with 12-fold symmetry as a background circle overlay, and smaller secondary mandalas at section corners. Add a vignette depth gradient overlay in the hero section to create a 3D immersive effect. Motifs should use ochre, maroon, and gold tones matching the temple palette.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in DarkCosmicTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with constellation line-art overlays: connected star-dot constellations spread across the hero and body using gold stroke lines and small circle nodes, plus a series of celestial arc rings (like orbital paths) centered behind the couple names. Add a radial starfield glow overlay for depth.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in FloralMarigoldTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with lush marigold garland border SVGs: a dense string-of-flowers garland border running along the top and bottom of the hero section, stylized individual marigold bloom icons scattered as section dividers, and a layered petal crown above the couple names. Add a warm bloom-glow overlay behind the hero text for depth.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in SouthIndianBronzeTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with Kolam-inspired dot-grid geometric patterns: a traditional dot-and-curve Kolam floor pattern as a faded full-width background overlay, and smaller Kolam border strips as section dividers. Add corner Kolam corner-knot motifs. Include a bronze metallic sheen radial gradient overlay in the hero for a 3D material depth effect.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in MinimalSanskritTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with refined Om calligraphy watermarks (a large semi-transparent Om symbol centered on the body background), delicate lotus line-art dividers between sections, and a subtle Devanagari-script floral border strip at the top and bottom of the hero. Keep all motifs minimal, monochromatic, and in ivory/gold tones. Add a soft vignette overlay for depth.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in SunsetBeachTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with stylized wave-crest SVG borders along the top and bottom of the hero, tropical foliage silhouettes (palm fronds and beach grass) as left/right edge accents, and a sun-burst radial halo SVG centered behind the couple names. Add a warm gradient depth glow in the hero for a 3D cinematic sunset feel.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in ThreeDFloatingDivineTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with divine lotus mandalas: a large radiant lotus SVG with concentric petals and a gold halo ring centered in the hero as a background glow layer, and layered semi-transparent lotus petal rings at increasing z-depths creating a 3D floating illusion. Add purple-to-gold radial glow overlays for immersive depth.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in MughalGardenTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with ornate Mughal arch frames (a pointed horseshoe arch outline used as a frame around the hero couple names area), a floral jali lattice pattern (geometric interlocking floral shapes in a grid) as a background tile overlay, and detailed corner rosette motifs in gold/emerald. Add a deep-shadow inner-frame vignette overlay for cinematic depth.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in VedicFireTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with agni flame SVGs (stylized fire tongue shapes rising from the bottom of the hero section), Sri Yantra-inspired geometric triangle nesting patterns (upward and downward interlocking triangles) as a centered background overlay, and a circular Vedic yantra ring frame around the couple names. Add an ember glow radial overlay in deep amber/crimson for 3D fire depth.

In `frontend/src/hooks/useQueries.ts` (and any hook that vends the backend actor), ensure the actor returned by `useActor` is not passed downstream until it is confirmed non-null. Any mutation hook (e.g., `useCreateInvite`, `useUpdateInvite`) must guard against a null/undefined actor and return a descriptive error to the caller if the actor is unavailable rather than throwing.

In `frontend/src/components/creator/StepPublish.tsx`, disable the Publish button and show a loading/spinner state while the actor is still initializing. Once the actor is ready, enable the button. This prevents users from clicking Publish before the backend client is available.

In StepPublish.tsx, replace the current actor readiness guard with a fully reactive implementation: subscribe to the actor reference via a useEffect or useMemo that watches the actor value from useActor; derive an `isActorReady` boolean that is only true when the actor is confirmed non-null and the Internet Identity session is active. Disable the 'Publish Invitation' button and show a 'Connecting to backend…' spinner/label whenever `isActorReady` is false. The publish handler must perform an explicit null-check on the actor at invocation time and abort with a user-facing inline error message if it is still null — it must never call the canister with a null actor.

new_feature_requests.summary: 8 richly styled Hindu templates with light/dark variants
Separate Bride and Groom photo upload buttons with original quality preservation and responsive srcset
Gallery multi-upload with drag & drop, reorder, and preview; no auto-compression of originals
Background music upload (MP3/OGG) with play/pause toggle; autoplay only after user interaction
Events section with animated timeline, multiple events (name, date/time, venue, address, Maps link button, optional image)
RSVP form (name, phone, email, attending, guest count, meal preference, message) with confetti/petal burst success animation and customizable thank-you text
OG meta tags: og:image (cover photo, 1200x630, cache-busted), og:title, og:description, twitter:card summary_large_image, fallback image if no cover uploaded
Live countdown timer (flip or glowing numeric) in hero; shows 'The celebration begins!' at zero
Cinematic visual effects: floating photo frames, 3D bobbing, mouse-tilt hero, parallax layers, animated mandala reveals, gold filigree masks, drifting petals/incense particles, glassmorphism panels
WYSIWYG text editing (fonts, sizes, color), toggleable sections, curated font pairings and color presets
Live preview (mobile + desktop), reduce-motion accessibility toggle, mobile-first responsive design
Creator flow: new invite → choose template → upload bride & groom → add events → add gallery/music → publish URL
8 seeded sample invites (one per template) with placeholder images/music

Create a Motoko backend actor that stores invite records anonymously. Each invite has a unique ID (nanoid-style), template ID, couple names, wedding date, cover image URL, bride photo URL, groom photo URL, gallery image URLs, background music URL, events list, RSVP responses, custom text fields, active sections config, theme variant (light/dark), and a createdAt timestamp. Expose endpoints: createInvite, getInvite(id), updateInvite(id, payload), submitRSVP(id, rsvpData), listSampleInvites. No authentication required.

Seed 8 sample invite records (one per template) in the backend on first deploy: Royal Rajasthani, Temple Mandala, Dark Cosmic, Floral Marigold, South Indian Bronze, Minimal Sanskrit, Sunset Beach, 3D Floating Divine. Each sample uses placeholder couple names, a future wedding date, placeholder image URLs pointing to /assets/generated/ files, and lorem-style event/venue data.

Update the Motoko backend to accept and store Base64-encoded photo strings (bride photo, groom photo, cover photo, gallery image URLs array) in invite records. Ensure the bridePhoto, groomPhoto, coverPhoto, and galleryImages fields in the InviteRecord type and the createInvite/updateInvite payload types can hold full Base64 data URL strings (data:image/...;base64,...) without truncation.

Implement a chunked image storage strategy in the Motoko backend to work around the 2MB IC ingress message limit. Instead of accepting full Base64 image data inline in createInvite/updateInvite calls, add a dedicated uploadImageChunk(inviteId, fieldKey, chunkIndex, totalChunks, chunkData) endpoint that accepts image data in chunks (each chunk ≤ 1MB). Add a finalizeImage(inviteId, fieldKey) endpoint that assembles the chunks and stores the complete image against the invite record. The invite record's image fields (bridePhoto, groomPhoto, coverPhoto, galleryImages array entries) should store the assembled data internally after finalization. createInvite and updateInvite payloads must no longer carry raw image data — image fields in those payloads should accept only an empty/placeholder value or be omitted entirely.
Fix bride and groom photos hidden behind overlay frames — photos should be visible inside decorative frames, not obscured by them
Music should autoplay on invitation page load (after first user interaction or immediately on open)
Fix slow publish and slow invitation page load times — optimize chunked upload speed and invite retrieval/rendering performance
Replace decorative overlay frame on bride/groom photos with a premium border/boundary around the photo (no frame overlapping in the middle of the photo)
Add a WhatsApp sharing button on the invitation page so guests can share the invite link via WhatsApp

In the Motoko backend main.mo, ensure the createInvite and updateInvite endpoints handle all required fields in the InvitePayload type without type mismatches or missing field errors that would cause the call to be rejected. Verify the return type of createInvite includes the newly created invite ID so the frontend can construct the shareable URL. Add defensive error handling so malformed payloads return a descriptive error rather than trapping silently.

In CreateInvitePage.tsx, audit the payload construction block that assembles the InvitePayload before calling createInvite/updateInvite. Ensure all required fields are present and correctly typed (no undefined values passed where the Motoko type expects a concrete value). Replace any remaining blob: or object URL references in the payload with empty strings or omit image fields entirely (since images are stored via chunked upload). Wrap the actor call in a try/catch and display a toast or inline error if it rejects, so the user is informed of any failure.

Build an RSVP responses page at /invite/:id/responses showing a summary (total attending, total guests, total responses) and a table of all respondents (name, attendance status, guest count, meal preference, message). Accessible to anyone with the invite ID. Add a link/button on the published invitation page pointing to the responses view. Style to match the existing cinematic Hindu wedding theme without touching existing functionality.

In the Motoko backend (backend/main.mo), add a getRSVPResponses(inviteId: Text) query endpoint that returns all RSVP submissions for a given invite ID. No authentication required — anyone with the invite ID can call it. The return value must include each respondent's full name, phone, email, attending status, guest count, meal preference, and message, plus the submission timestamp.
Implement Internet Identity login — only authenticated users can create/edit invites and view RSVP responses; anonymous invite creation is disabled
Home/landing page transforms into a personal 'My Invitations' dashboard when the user is logged in, showing their invites with Edit and View RSVP Responses options per invite
'View RSVP Responses' button on the published invitation page is hidden from guests — only visible to the logged-in creator of that specific invite
All invite management (create, edit, view RSVP responses) requires login; old anonymous invites are no longer editable
Make the email field in the RSVP form optional (not required)
Add 4 new premium templates: Mughal Garden, Vedic Fire, Royal Navy Zari, and Lotus Bloom — each with light/dark variants and a Premium badge

Update the Motoko backend (backend/main.mo) to associate every invite with the authenticated caller's principal. Add a `creatorPrincipal` field to the InviteRecord type. On `createInvite`, store `caller` as the `creatorPrincipal`. On `updateInvite`, reject the call (return an error) if `caller` does not match the stored `creatorPrincipal`. Add a `getMyInvites()` query that returns all invites owned by the caller principal. Add a `getInviteCreator(inviteId: Text)` query that returns the creator principal for a given invite ID so the frontend can determine ownership. Keep `getInvite`, `submitRSVP`, and the public read paths unauthenticated. Reject `createInvite` if the caller is the anonymous principal.

Add a `getRSVPResponses` backend endpoint authorization check: only the creator principal of an invite may call `getRSVPResponses(inviteId)`. Return an access-denied error if the caller is not the creator. Update the backend `main.mo` accordingly.
Fix RSVP section text visibility — text color blends into the background making it unreadable; update input/label/textarea/dropdown text colors for strong contrast across all templates

Fix edit flow not pre-filling saved invite data — when clicking Edit from the dashboard, all form fields are empty; initialize form state with saved invite data (couple details, events, template choice, all fields) fetched from the backend

Give each of the 12 templates its own palette-matched RSVP section background color (e.g., deep maroon for Royal Rajasthani, dark navy for Royal Navy Zari, emerald for Mughal Garden, etc.) so form labels and inputs are clearly readable

Fully redesign SVG decorative motifs for all 12 templates so each has unique per-template patterns (e.g., filigree jali for Royal Rajasthani, concentric mandala for Temple Mandala, constellations for Dark Cosmic, marigold garlands for Floral Marigold, Kolam patterns for South Indian Bronze, Om watermark for Minimal Sanskrit, waves for Sunset Beach, lotus halos for 3D Floating Divine, arch jali for Mughal Garden, agni yantra for Vedic Fire, zari embroidery for Royal Navy Zari, water-lily petals for Lotus Bloom) plus overlay depth/3D effects to make templates richer and more immersive
Update RoyalNavyZariTemplate.tsx to pass a deep navy blue tint (e.g., rgba(5, 10, 60, 0.92)) as the RSVP section background that fits its royal navy/gold zari palette.

Update LotusBloomTemplate.tsx to pass a deep rose/dusty pink tint (e.g., rgba(110, 30, 60, 0.88)) as the RSVP section background that fits its soft pink/lotus palette, ensuring form labels and inputs remain readable.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in RoyalNavyZariTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with gold zari thread-stitch embroidery border SVGs: a densely stitched gold zari border strip running along all four edges of the hero section with a chevron/diamond stitch pattern, a regal crest/crown motif centered at the top of the hero, and small zari floral medallions as section dividers. Add a gold metallic sheen radial overlay in the hero for material depth.

Fully redesign the SVG decorative motifs in LotusBloomTemplate.tsx. Replace existing patterns with layered water-lily petal overlays: large multi-layered lotus flower SVGs with concentric petal rings fading from rose to soft white, used as background and section divider elements, a serene ripple-ring water surface SVG below the lotus blooms suggesting a pond surface, and delicate single floating petal silhouettes scattered across the layout. Add a soft pink radial bloom glow in the hero for 3D depth.

Fix 'Actor not available' error when clicking Publish Invitation — ensure the backend actor/canister client is fully initialized before the publish call is made, add a readiness check, and surface a clear error if the actor is unavailable

In `frontend/src/pages/CreateInvitePage.tsx`, add a readiness guard before the publish canister call: check that the actor instance is non-null and fully initialized before proceeding. If the actor is not yet available when the user clicks Publish, display a clear inline error message ('Backend not ready — please wait a moment and try again') and do not attempt the canister call. Do not silently fail or hang.

In CreateInvitePage.tsx, add a top-level actor readiness guard in the handlePublish function: before assembling the InvitePayload or making any canister call, check that the actor instance is strictly non-null. If the actor is null at this point, immediately abort the publish flow, display a clear inline error message to the user ('Backend connection unavailable — please wait a moment and try again'), and return early. This guard is a second line of defense in addition to the button-level guard in StepPublish.tsx.

In frontend/src/hooks/useQueries.ts, audit all mutation hooks (useCreateInvite, useUpdateInvite, and any other hook that calls the actor). Ensure each mutation function: (1) receives the actor as a dependency that is watched reactively, (2) performs an explicit non-null assertion on the actor before any canister call, and (3) throws or returns a descriptive error object (e.g., { error: 'Actor not initialized' }) if the actor is null — rather than throwing a generic JS error or calling a null reference. This ensures the error propagates gracefully to the calling component.
